title: ThinkPath Starter Pack 01
description: Original branching scenarios that train strategy selection, problem solving style, and metacognitive correction. Built for upload into Lovable.

step_1_choices.description: Rewrite 25 as 100/4
step_1_choices.feedback: This is the strongest opening because it reduces the work.

step_1_choices.description: Estimate first, then decide whether exact work is needed.
step_1_choices.feedback: Estimation is useful for direction, but you still need an exact path.

step_1_choices.description: Start calculating randomly and hope a pattern appears.
step_1_choices.feedback: Unplanned work can waste time and hide structure.

step_1_choices.description: Use the standard algorithm step by step.
step_1_choices.feedback: This will work, but it may take longer than necessary.

choices.description: Take the simplification all the way before doing extra work.
choices.feedback: Excellent. You kept the efficient strategy.

choices.description: Switch back to a longer method because it feels safer.
choices.feedback: You can still finish, but you gave up efficiency.

choices.description: Estimate only and stop there.
choices.feedback: Estimation helps, but it does not complete the task.

choices.description: Restart from scratch with a random operation.
choices.feedback: That breaks the strategy chain.

path_1_B.step_prompt: How can you improve this path?

choices.description: Use the idea to narrow the possibilities or simplify the work.
choices.feedback: Nice recovery. You refined a decent opening into a strong path.

choices.description: Keep going without checking whether there is a cleaner move.
choices.feedback: You may finish, but you may miss a better route.

choices.description: Abandon the plan and guess.
choices.feedback: Guessing throws away useful progress.

choices.description: Add unrelated work to feel productive.
choices.feedback: Extra steps are not the same as good reasoning.

path_1_C.step_prompt: You feel stuck. What should happen next?

choices.description: Step back and choose a strategy based on structure or evidence.
choices.feedback: Excellent reset. Better framing improves the whole attempt.

choices.description: Keep pushing randomly because stopping feels slow.
choices.feedback: Persistence matters, but direction matters more.

choices.description: Copy the wording of the problem without thinking.
choices.feedback: Repeating the prompt does not advance the solution.

choices.description: Assume the hardest-looking path must be correct.
choices.feedback: Difficulty is not evidence.

path_1_D.step_prompt: What is the smartest adjustment now?

choices.description: Pause and look for structure before doing more work.
choices.feedback: Good correction. You shifted from brute force to strategy.

choices.description: Double down on the long path even though it is messy.
choices.feedback: It may succeed, but it remains inefficient.

choices.description: Skip the hard part and choose an answer by feel.
choices.feedback: That increases error risk.

choices.description: Rewrite the problem in a more confusing way.
choices.feedback: Complexity is not progress.

final_summary.coach_tip: Strong problem solvers do not just work harder; they choose a smarter entry point and adjust quickly if a path becomes inefficient.

choices.feedback: Good correction. You shifted from brute force to strategy.

choices.description: Double down on the long path even though it is messy.
choices.feedback: It may succeed, but it remains inefficient.

choices.description: Skip the hard part and choose an answer by feel.
choices.feedback: That increases error risk.

choices.description: Rewrite the problem in a more confusing way.
choices.feedback: Complexity is not progress.

choices.description: Take the simplification all the way before doing extra work.
choices.feedback: Excellent. You kept the efficient strategy.

choices.description: Switch back to a longer method because it feels safer.
choices.feedback: You can still finish, but you gave up efficiency.

choices.description: Estimate only and stop there.
choices.feedback: Estimation helps, but it does not complete the task.

choices.description: Restart from scratch with a random operation.
choices.feedback: That breaks the strategy chain.

final_summary.best_strategy: Estimate to see whether it is reasonable
final_summary.coach_tip: Strong problem solvers do not just work harder; they choose a smarter entry point and adjust quickly if a path becomes inefficient.

scenarios.title: Decision Strategy 10
scenarios.problem: You have two strategies: one reliable and one faster but riskier.

step_1_choices.description: Gather a small amount of key evidence, then decide.
step_1_choices.feedback: This balances action with information.

step_1_choices.description: Wait and hope the problem resolves itself.
step_1_choices.feedback: Sometimes waiting helps, but it is usually not the strongest plan.

step_1_choices.description: Match the strategy to the stakes and time limit
step_1_choices.feedback: Strong decisions begin with a clean frame.

step_1_choices.description: Choose the fastest-looking option immediately.
step_1_choices.feedback: Speed without framing can backfire.

choices.description: Keep going without checking whether there is a cleaner move.

choices.description: Abandon the plan and guess.
choices.feedback: Guessing throws away useful progress.

choices.description: Add unrelated work to feel productive.
